title: Quiz: Grover's Algorithm and Symmetric Cryptography
description: Test your understanding of Grover's algorithm and its impact on symmetric cryptography

questions.question: What type of speedup does Grover's algorithm provide?

options.text: Logarithmic - from O(N) to O(log N)

questions.explanation: Grover's algorithm provides a quadratic speedup, reducing search complexity from O(N) to O(√N). This is significant but less dramatic than Shor's exponential speedup.

questions.question: What is the post-quantum security level of AES-128?
questions.explanation: Grover's algorithm effectively halves the bit security. AES-128 with 128-bit classical security has only 64-bit post-quantum security (2^64 quantum operations needed).

questions.question: What key size should you use for 128-bit post-quantum security with AES?
questions.explanation: For 128-bit post-quantum security, you need a 256-bit key (256/2 = 128). This is the 'double your key size' rule for quantum resistance.

questions.question: How does Grover's algorithm affect hash function preimage resistance?

options.text: It completely breaks all hash functions
It halves the bit security (n-bit → n/2-bit)
It has no effect on hash functions
It reduces security by a factor of 3

questions.explanation: Grover's algorithm can be used to find preimages, reducing n-bit preimage resistance to n/2 bits. SHA-256's 256-bit preimage resistance becomes 128-bit post-quantum.

questions.question: Unlike Shor's algorithm, Grover's algorithm doesn't require replacing existing algorithms—just using larger parameters.
questions.explanation: True. Grover's only weakens security; it doesn't break the underlying math. The mitigation is simple: use larger keys (256-bit for symmetric) and larger hash outputs. No new algorithms needed.

questions.question: Why is AES-256 still considered secure against quantum computers?

options.text: Quantum computers cannot run Grover's algorithm
2^128 quantum operations is still computationally infeasible
Grover's algorithm doesn't apply to block ciphers

questions.explanation: Even with Grover's quadratic speedup, breaking AES-256 requires 2^128 quantum operations. This is still astronomically large and infeasible with any foreseeable technology.

questions.question: What is the quantum collision resistance of SHA-256?
questions.explanation: Quantum collision attacks use the BHT algorithm, which provides O(2^(n/3)) complexity. For SHA-256, this is approximately 85 bits of quantum collision resistance.

questions.question: Which of the following are TRUE about Grover's algorithm? (Select all that apply)

options.text: It provides quadratic speedup for unstructured search
The mitigation is to use longer keys
It can be parallelized effectively for additional speedup

questions.explanation: Grover's provides quadratic speedup (a), and the mitigation is longer keys (c). It doesn't completely break AES-128—it reduces security to 64 bits, which is weak but not broken. Grover's cannot be parallelized effectively; running k copies only provides a √k speedup.
